instructions: For questions 29 to 40, write your answers in this booklet. The number of marks available is shown in brackets [ ] at the end of each question or part question.

questions.context: The diagram shows a human body system (circulatory system) with part Y labelled (heart).

parts.question: Identify the system and state the function of this system.
Name Y.
Other than Y, name another two parts of this system.

questions.context: The diagram shows fruits E and F. (E has a feathery/parachute structure; F is a pod that splits open.)

parts.question: How are the seeds of E and F dispersed?
State one advantage of the method used by F to disperse its seeds.

parts.context: John conducted an experiment to find out if the amount of wind affects the distance moved by E using a fan. John repeated the experiment five times with all factors kept the same except for one.
parts.question: Suggest two possible ways of varying this factor.

questions.context: The diagram shows how substances are transported in a plant. P and Q represent different parts of the plant. The arrows represent the movement of substances G and H (G = dashed arrow; H = solid arrow). Roots → P → Q; P → Flowers.

parts.question: Identify parts P and Q.
Identify substances represented by H and describe how these substances move in the plant.

questions.context: Malaria-related experiment. (a) Oil is sprayed on stagnant water with mosquito larvae. (b) Malaria is a disease spread by mosquito A. Children infected with malaria are more attractive to mosquito A due to a certain smell that they produce. Joel used the set-up shown with 20 of mosquito A in box P (with stirring and a white shirt worn by a child infected with malaria) and box Q (with white shirt worn by an uninfected child). He counted the number of times mosquitoes landed on the shirt for 3 minutes, then repeated with another shirt.

parts.question: Explain how this prevents mosquito from breeding.

subparts.question: State a hypothesis on how the smell on a shirt affects its attractiveness to mosquito A.
Joel used a white shirt instead of a black shirt for the experiment. Suggest why using a white shirt allows him to obtain more accurate results.

subparts.context: Joel recorded his results: Shirt in box P - 300 landings; Shirt in box Q - 120 landings. Scientists are able to store the smell produced by infected people in a container.
subparts.question: Suggest how Joel could use this smell and his results to reduce the number of bites on people.

questions.context: Bees are commonly found near flowering plants.

parts.question: Describe how bees help plants to reproduce.

parts.context: Jia Xuan investigated the effect of substance N on bees with two containers, R and S, of the same size using a circular cage. Container R: sugar water only. Container S: sugar water and substance N. Jia Xuan introduced bees into the cage and closed the opening. She counted the number of times bees visited each container for a duration of 3 hours on Day 1, Day 5 and Day 10.
parts.question: Suggest how the arrangement of containers in the cage allowed for a fair test.

parts.context: Bar chart shows: container R visits about equal on Day 1, 5, 10; container S visits decrease over time.
parts.question: Describe how substance N affected the number of times bees visited both containers from Day 1 to Day 10.

questions.context: Jayden placed the same amount of pond water which contained tiny organisms into two identical glass containers, A and B. He painted container B black. He placed both containers at the same place under bright light. He measured the amount of oxygen in each container daily. Graph shows: container A oxygen increases over days; container B oxygen decreases over days.

parts.question: Explain why the amount of oxygen in container A increased while the amount of oxygen in container B decreased.
Suggest one possible reason that could have resulted in Jayden obtaining inaccurate results.

questions.context: Sylvia boiled some water in a pot on a stove.

parts.question: State what boiling means.

parts.context: Sylvia wanted to cook some eggs at 80 °C. When the water boiled, she removed the pot from the stove and added some cold water. She then put in some eggs and covered the pot for 10 minutes. Diagram shows a temperature sensor reading 80 °C with the pot on a metal tabletop.
parts.question: Without reheating the pot, suggest two ways that Sylvia can make sure that water was kept at about 80 °C in the same pot.

questions.context: Ravi rolled a metal ball from point X at a low speed past a strong magnet on a table. The ball moved past the magnet in a straight line (Diagram 1).

parts.question: Explain why the metal ball moved past the magnet in a straight line.

parts.context: He next rolled an iron ball from X in the same way. Possible paths shown: Q, R, S, T (Q goes away from magnet, R goes towards and past magnet, S curves towards magnet, T curves away from magnet).
parts.question: Which path of the iron ball, Q, R, S or T, is most likely? Explain your answer.

questions.context: Aisha tied a mass to an electric toy train (Diagram 1). The train moved slowly from A to B. Next, Aisha linked a wagon to the train (Diagram 2). When she placed the same mass on the wagon, the train moved faster from A to B.

parts.question: Give a reason why the train moved faster when pulling the mass on the wagon.
The train moved from A to B easily but moved up the slope slowly. Explain why the train moved up the slope slowly.

parts.context: The train and wagon were linked by plastic rings (Diagram 3). When the wagon was on the slope, it broke loose from the train and rolled downwards (Diagram 4). The train continued to move up the slope without the plastic rings being broken.
parts.question: Explain why the wagon broke loose from the train on the slope without breaking the plastic rings.

questions.context: Ahmad set up an experiment in a room as shown. There was no wind. He released the wooden strip with plasticine at A gently. The plasticine made one complete swing when it moved from A to B and back to A. He wanted to investigate how the mass of plasticine affects the time taken for ten complete swings. His results: 10 g - 15.0 s; 15 g - 14.8 s; 20 g - 15.2 s; 25 g - 14.9 s.

parts.question: State two variables he needed to keep constant for the experiment.
Based on his results above, state how the mass of plasticine affects the time for ten complete swings.
Suggest one improvement to the experiment to obtain more accurate results.

questions.context: Peter built a door lock as shown. When he closed the switch, the iron bolt moved to the right, away from the catch and the door was unlocked. The set-up has: door, catch (fixed to door), wall, iron bolt, wire coiled around a fixed metal cylinder, spring, battery and switch.

parts.question: State why copper, a type of metal, is used to make wires.
Explain how the door was unlocked when Peter closed the switch.

When Peter opened the switch again, he found that the bolt did not go deep enough into the catch to lock the door properly. Without changing the iron bolt, suggest changes to his set-up so that he can lock and unlock the door properly. Explain your answer.

questions.context: John placed a pack of brown sausages wrapped in clear plastic from the freezer onto a table. After a short time, a thin layer of white solid was formed on the surface of the plastic wrapping.

parts.question: Explain how the white solid was formed.
The white solid cannot be seen after a while. Explain why.
